article.description: Freshman Ben Gordon came up big again in his second Big East tournament game, this time leading Connecticut to an 82-77 victory over Notre Dame on Friday night and a berth in the championship game.
article.headline: Huskies reach ninth Big East final
article.linkText: Freshman beast: Gordon lifts UConn into Big East finals
article.story:
NEW YORK (AP) -- Connecticut used its staple -- defense -- and its
latest star freshman -- Ben Gordon -- to advance to the Big East
tournament championship game for the sixth time in eight years.

	   Gordon came up big again in his second Big East tournament game,
this time leading the 19th-ranked Huskies (No. 24 ESPN/USA Today, No. 19 AP)to an 82-77 victory over
Notre Dame on Friday night in the semifinals.

	   The Huskies (23-6) will play No. 7 Pittsburgh, which beat No. 20
Miami 76-71, for the title on Saturday night, the first time the
league's division champions will meet this season.

	   "We were the kings of our division and they were the kings of
theirs so it's only right for us to meet in the championship
game," said Caron Butler, who led the Huskies with 20 points.

	   The win was the Huskies' eighth straight and it will be their
ninth appearance in the championship game, all under Jim Calhoun.
They have won four titles, the last in 1999 when they went on to
win the national championship.

	   "We think the reason it's happened is because we have great
players," Calhoun said.

	   And the latest of that group is Gordon, whose 23 points in the
72-70 quarterfinal victory over Villanova included the game-winning
3-pointer with 11 seconds left. He had 19 points and six assists
Friday.

	   He scored four points and had an assist on the closing basket of
the Huskies' 10-0 run that broke open the close game and made it
69-59 with 6:35 to play.

	   Connecticut's defense was exceptional during the run, holding
Notre Dame (21-10) scoreless for 6:50, a span of eight possessions.

	   "We led the conference in field-goal percentage defense over 28
games," Calhoun said. "What we really are is a very good
defensive team. Our identity is stopping you and then getting
shots."

	   Gordon made five of the 10 shots he took, including two in the
big run.

	   "We needed a little offensive spark in the last seven
minutes," he said. "I realized I hadn't scored in a while."

	   Calhoun not only credited Gordon, but the team's veterans.

	   "Ben was terrific, but I always look to the leaders," Calhoun
said. "He's no longer a freshman. He's had the leadership, through
the other players, to bring him along."

	   Johnnie Selvie had 16 points for Connecticut.

	   Freshman Chris Thomas had 24 points and 10 assists for the
Fighting Irish, including scoring their last 11 points over the
final three minutes. Ryan Humphrey had 21 points and 16 rebounds,
and Matt Carroll had 20 points for Notre Dame, which beat St.
John's 83-63 in the quarterfinals and made its first appearance in
the semifinals.

	   "This was new territory for us as a program," Fighting Irish
coach Mike Brey said. "I think we tried to play too fast some
times but that came from the atmosphere, and their quickness had
something to do with it."

	   Notre Dame and Connecticut hadn't played each other this season
and the teams were even for the first 28 minutes, until the Huskies
went on the run.

	   Gordon hit a fadeaway jumper and added a driving floater for a
67-59 lead, and he made a nice bounce pass to Selvie for the layup
that capped the run. Gordon's third 3-pointer gave Connecticut its
biggest lead, 74-63, with 3:51 to play.

	   Thomas, who was 6-for-9 on 3-pointers, hit one with 57 seconds
left and then added two free throws 20 seconds later to get Notre
Dame within 78-74, but the Huskies went 5-for-6 from the free-throw
line over the final 33 seconds to offset Thomas' final 3 of the
game.

	   "We got a little caught up in the atmosphere and in the second
half Connecticut took control of the tempo," said Thomas, the
conference freshman of the year who had 15 points and 11 rebounds
in the quarterfinals.

	   Humphrey, a senior, wasn't surprised at how the two freshmen
played before a sellout crowd of 19,528 at Madison Square Garden.

	   "Chris came here with all the hype and has lived up to it,"
Humphrey said. "Ben Gordon was highly touted, too, and I was
impressed he shot with as much confidence as he did."
